title: Humanoids Cost as Much as an SUV Now | Nikhil Kamath x Brett Adcock | WTF Online Ep 2

chunks.text: Let me know when we guys can start.
Ready?
Would you leave your kids with your humanoid?
We're not there today.
Like, I would not let my robot roam free
for hours and weeks, right now, with my young kids.
Everybody talks about China being so far ahead in robotics.
Why is that?
Yeah, I just don't believe this.
Hi, Brett, lovely to meet you.
What's behind you? Maybe we can start there.
Yeah, so I'm at Figure Offices.
We design humanoid robots.
So, these are robots that, kind of, move around the world like humans.
Legs, arms, hands.
Behind me is our first generation robot,
second and third generation humanoid robots here at Figure.
Okay. So, tell us a bit about yourself, Brett.
Where did you begin? Where were you born?
How did you come to build a humanoid?
Yeah. So, I was born in the Midwest here in the U.S.
on a third generation farm, actually.
We farmed corn and soybeans.
And then, basically, since middle school and high school,
I started working on technology businesses and mostly in software.
And that led to, basically now, 20 years building tech companies.
I spent about 10 years in software.
Ended up starting a company in 2012 called Vettery.
And it was an AI marketplace for recruiting.
The goal is to basically help
candidates find jobs and employers find great talent.
And that whole process is extremely broken and hard.
I ended up selling Vettery
for a little over 100 million in 2017 to the Adecco Group,
which is the largest recruiting company in the world.
After that, I really wanted to get into hardware.
So I started a company called Archer Aviation in 2018.
And what we do is we build electric aircraft,
electric vertical take-off and landing aircraft.
So it's kind of like a-- it takes off, kind of, like a helicopter,
so you can put them in cities.
And it flies a little bit like an airplane and it's a fully electric powertrain.
And the goal is to basically help move traffic that's in cities
and move it into the air, so folks can get around a lot easier.
And yeah, basically designed five generations of aircraft.
So far at Archer, our current aircraft is Midnight.
It's a piloted four-passenger aircraft that we're flying now.
Trying to get that certified in the U.S. airspace, the FAA.
Ended up taking that company public about four years ago, five years ago.
And then, yeah, three years ago, I started Figure.
So we design and manufacture humanoid robots.
And the goal here at Figure is to be able to do
everything a human can in the physical world.
I have an investment in a company here in India,
which does electric taxis, called Sarla Aviation.
How far are we, really? Traffic is a big problem in India.
And I don't know how different Archer is 'cause I've not seen enough about it.
But it's the typical electric taxi,
160 kilometres range on a charge,
meant to take off from hotels.
The helipads on top of a hotel get to the airport.
How far are we from a world where this could be more pragmatic?
This is something that will be used by people.
I mean, we have aircraft today that are flying fully piloted.
You have a couple of components here.
The first is you have to be in the right decade
to make sure the technologies are all feasible to make this really happen.
This is a really hard technology.
Most of the problems and challenges here happen because we have a...
There's probably like a 30X difference in energy density
between traditional fossil fuel, say, kerosene,
that you might find in an aircraft, in an electric aircraft.
Like what we find in batteries.
There's a problem of building that technology and really making it viable.
I think we've shown at Archer that that is fully possible today.
I mean, we fly basically every week.
Our full-scale aircraft at this point, fully electric,
similar concept operations we'd want to do in market.
At this point, it's largely a safety and certification and policy--
basically, what's gating this is certification.
In most countries, it's governed federally where you need.
Like, in Europe, it's EASA, here in the U.S., it's FAA.
You need, basically, a certification level
at the federal level that unblocks your ability
to take passengers and make it ubiquitous.
So at this point, largely missing certification,
you didn't have manufacturing at scale needed.
But the technology is certainly here.
So I think within the next five years,
we'll be taking passengers, paying passengers around
in airspaces here in the world on electric aircraft like this.
And if that's true for America, do you think that will be the way forward?
Do you think that it'll replace...
human-driven taxis in the near future?
I don't think it replaces all human-driven taxis.
I think it just takes a large portion of what's--
Basically, the folks that are taking transportation today that might be going--
that might be spending like half an hour, hour, hour and a half in traffic,
which is sizeable. In some places like L.A.,
it's almost 10% of all the travel in the mornings and after work.
Just a much better quality of life, if we're not sitting in traffic all day.
Would it also change short haul flights?
Say, between San Francisco and L.A., for example, which is one hour?
My view is all aircraft, all aviation,
all air transport will move electric here.
And I think, hopefully, in our lifetime.
It's starting out with very short-range flights,
mostly because of a specific battery energy density issue.
We have, like--
If you had to compare a battery
versus what's in an aircraft, say, kerosene,
there was basically a third-- roughly a 30X difference
in net energy in those two systems.
So you basically have an issue where
you can basically just go 30X, less far.
And then, with traditional, with eVTOL aircraft, you're taking off vertically,
you're using a considerable amount of power doing that
versus, like I said, an aircraft that might be going
from San Francisco to L.A. that might be taking off conventionally.
But we're starting with-- We're starting now with these short--
the kind of shorter flights, helping them move passengers around.
It'll then happen regionally,
and then it'll happen at much larger, longer distances.
The technologies needed to go much longer distances
are very different than what we're designing
here today at Archer with eVTOL aircraft.
To get to those level distances where you need to go city to city,
travelling distances, you're gonna have to redesign
a different types of aircraft system to do that, in my mind.
Will that happen, though, do you think?
It'll happen for sure, yeah.
For sure, it'll happen.
So if you could bet on a train, a car,
a traditional plane or an EV flying vehicle,
going between San Francisco and L.A.,
you would bet on a flying electric vehicle?
Electric gets you-- I mean, we can design
some of the safest forms of transportation in the world, like flying in the air.
I mean, flying today, we're--
it's the safest form of transportation we take as humans.
There will be a large portion of folks
that will travel between those cities like this, in the air.
I think there will also be a large portion of that
that also travels on the ground, whether it's train or tunnel or car.
There's so many humans now, and so many humans are just moving into cities.
So you just have a real issue where the infrastructure
is not just able to support this at this moment.
Right.
And how did you transition from Archer to Figure AI?
Could you tell us about that?
Well, I think one thing is interesting at Archer
is the aircraft is basically like a flying robot.
And if you think about what we do at Figure building robots,
you need batteries and motors
and embedded systems, like sensors, control software,
a lot of the same kind of ingredients needed
to kind of build any robot, just in general.
So having built up Archer over the years,
got really familiar with those key areas
and integrated hardware and software together just overall.
I mean, for me as an entrepreneur, working in hardware,
I think the humanoid robotics,
or solving general purpose humanoids,
is the ultimate meta problem.
I think the end state for most things done in the physical world
is done through a humanoid robot, at this point.
I think we'll see billions of humanoids on the planet in our lifetime,
and they will do everything for us, from things at home,
like doing the laundry, washing dishes,
unpacking groceries, cleaning up the house to--
We will see billions in the commercial workforce,
in areas of logistics and manufacturing and healthcare.
Our core thesis here is that the world was designed for humans.
We, as humans, designed it for ourselves.
So everything you do, we do all day is...
is designed for the way we look and feel.
And the whole world was for it.
Even we're drinking coffees and teas, right?
With the cups that we hold and the door that we exit from here
and the stairs and all the tools we use
were all designed for the human form.
So our belief here is that the humanoid
is like the ultimate general purpose machine.
And...
So what we have here is, we have a deep focus
on designing really incredible hardware systems.
At Figure here, we design the entire hardware
and software stack on the robot, almost entirely ourselves, at this point.
So that goes down to the motors and battery systems,
all the electronics, wiring,
kinematics, and structures and sensors.
And then, two, is we spend a large amount of time on the AI side.
This is an incredibly hard problem.
If you think about a humanoid robot, the ones you see behind me,
every robot has about 40 different joints roughly, on the robot,
and a joint has a motor that can spin roughly 360 degrees.
So the amount of positions the body can be in,
is equal to 360 to the power of 40.
And so that means that there's more potential states
the robot can be in than atoms in the universe,
which is like always crazy to think about, but it's like much more.
So you can't solve this with code.
You have to use advanced AI neural nets,
to be able to make this really work well.
So we spend a large fraction of our time outside of hardware
on how do we get neural networks to control the robot
and do the things we would need to do, like real stuff that humans would do.
We're about three and a half years old now.
We've shipped now three generations of humanoid robots,
and we've been able to demonstrate that humanoids
can do useful human-like work, now, fully autonomously,
and, in most cases, with neural nets all the way down the stack.
I thought of this recently.
I didn't realise how close we were to humanoids being in the actual world.
And when I thought about that, I'm like,
"Wow, I should have really looked this up earlier,
researched it more," because it feels very,
I don't know, dystopian or utopian to
picture a world where I have something that looks like me
walking around my house,
probably sitting in my office and in the car, sitting beside me.
It's kind of a weird feeling to get your head around.
Maybe 'cause I'm not used to it and I will get used to it soon.
Yeah, I think it just feels like such a sci-fi movie a bit,
when you see these things working, it feels just so--
It almost feels like it's not possible to go do and make it work.
We have them walking around here in the office
and it's still not used to it. It looks incredible.
Seeing robots out and about, just walking all day and doing stuff.
Does it feel scary, Brett?
Not to us, no.
We feel really energised being around the robots and seeing it. Not scary.
I mean, we built them, we really understand the limitations and...
we've inherently tried to intrinsically design
the hardware for safety and also the whole system for safety.
So not scary to us.
We're around them all day.
We've made them, we birthed them, you know, we brought them here.
So it's something really special about it where it doesn't feel like it's possible.
It should be like 20 years out in the future,
but somehow it's like they're here and they're working really well.
I think the robots we have are working really well.
And to see that happen, you're like, "Oh man, just give us 5 or 10 more years.
What would that really look like?"
It feels like it's just too soon for the world, a bit.
And... but it's all happening now.
We're really spending a lot of time trying to build
true general purposeness, like be able to talk to a robot
and have it do most useful things you'd want it to do.
That's like a really hard problem.
It's largely a software and AI problem at this point, for us to go solve,
but we can do it.
We announced recently, Figure 03,
our third generation robots right behind me.
And we-- we put out a bunch of content
about how the robots doing a bunch of stuff
in homes and even in the workforce.
And the robot is doing all those tasks.
It's doing all the manipulation for all those tasks and actions,
all through a neural network.
And we did maybe a dozen, dozen and a half
or so use cases there and under-- in a few weeks--
with neural nets at places the robots never seen
a month before that.
And that's just like... that's the ingredient
you really need to scale at really large levels.
You need neural nets on humanoid hardware, and we're seeing that now.
So it just shows us how close we are.
I know it's not something we'll solve in a month,
but it's certainly not something that's 10 years away, at this point.
Can we start with you breaking down a robot, Brett?
What's inside?
I think the first thing you could maybe start with is vision.
'Cause I did a little bit of research on vision and how a robot sees
and how it has spatial intelligence, in a way,
but would love to hear from you.
Like, I can see the robot behind you, what's in it?
Yeah, let me start with the basics of the robot
and build up to this, in terms of vision.
Vision is kind of higher level in the stack, low level on the robot.
It's basically a bunch of motors.
And we have roughly 40 of them on the robot.
We call them actuators,
but they're similar electric motors that we would see out in the world today.
We have a bunch more sensors and transmission and other things in there
that make it much more complicated,
but you can think of every joint as having an electric motor inside of it,
that is getting commands from a central computer
on how to output torque or how to basically move joint positions.
That is powered by a battery system,
basically a traditional lithium ion battery pack
that you might see in electric car or electric aircraft.
And where does the battery sit?
Yeah, it's right here in the middle of the torso on the chest.
So we have a bunch of motors,
we have a battery system in the torso
and then we have a computer, both a CPU for compute
and we have a GPU for inference for our AI models,
sitting in the torso next to the battery system.
And how much of the compute happens physically on the robot
and how much happens outside of the robot?
Today, all of our compute happens on the robot.
So we do everything onboard.
Will that change tomorrow?
Some of the compute will start moving more and more off-board.
There is naturally some compute that needs to be done here onboard.
We need to run a decent amount of the neural nets,
kind of like...
basically at a couple hundred Hertz, a couple hundred times a second.
There's no way you're gonna be able to do that fully off-board.
The robot would just be too slow.
Generally off-board,
you can maybe get 2 to 10 Hertz or something like that.
You generally communicate a few times a second,
but we need 200 times a second minimum, onboard the robot.
So we have a lot of onboard compute today,
sits on the robot to tell it how to move its motors and limbs, really fast speeds.
You gotta think like, every little time second,
the robot is repositioning its entire body
to balance, it's repositioning its hand and fingers,
maybe head's moving to start seeing some better.
And it's doing that closed loop, meaning based on where the hand's going
and what the task is doing, the robot's reevaluating what to go do.
And it's doing that a couple hundred times a second.
So we have, naturally, a lot of compute here.
We will off-board-- we can off-board more and more
of the bigger brain thinking stuff over time into the cloud.
But today we do it all onboard, mostly because we don't need a...
we don't have to worry about having a network connection,
you don't need Wi-Fi or 5G in this case,
and we can operate at really fast speeds and we know we can do the work.
A human-- Well, I guess some humans can't work without a phone
or a network, but humans really
can do stuff without a real Internet connection.
And that's similar to how you would think about a robot too,
it'd be a bummer if the Wi-Fi went out and the robot can't do anything anymore.
So our compute lives onboard.
We have a ton of wiring, a ton of distributed electronics,
and then we have a bunch of sensors inside the motors
and the rest of the robot to understand
how to balance and feel forces and track positions.
So, traditional robots that you see out in the world today,
if you put your hand harder and harder on the table
and keep pushing, while not moving,
the robots really can't really understand what's really happening.
Our robot can feel that force, like a human could.
We have, basically, torque tracking
and force feedback in every joint across the whole robot.
And that's really important to help balance,
and we have to touch the world and go out there and grab things.
We really need to understand those forces.
That's also a tricky sensor to integrate into the robot here, internally.
We have cameras, so we see in pixel space.
On this current generation, we have, I think, eight cameras.
We have one on each palm of the hands,
and then we have them in the head, the rest,
so we can see around ourselves.
So the robot sees through a camera.
That camera can update pretty quickly,
and that feeds into an onboard computer,
that then ultimately tells the motors what to do.
So that's roughly what's really happening.
So the robot's reasoning in pixel space.
It's going around like a self-driving car.
We don't have LIDAR, so like a camera-based self-driving car,
and we're reasoning through the world based on that, to do work.
I'm no expert at this,
but if one were to say the Moore's law is starting to fail,
where the number of transistors you can put on a chipset
will not...
increase at the pace that we have seen,
they won't continue to double
long-term to increase compute onboard...
Is that possible to have enough compute onboard
to make the robot never need to connect to a network, per se?
Yeah, we run fully onboard today in commercial settings
every day right now without a network.
So we have a commercial customer here in the U.S.
we've now been running with for over five months now,
every day...
We put this out publicly, it's with BMW.
That's a fully onboard system that's doing
all the reasoning onboard of what the robot should do.
What you're seeing in the world is,
even if Moore's Law slows down, compute's getting better,
so you'll be able to put more on compute over time than you have before.
So our current Figure 03 robot has more compute,
better compute than we had in previous generations.
Second thing is AI models that we're putting on
are getting smarter, and it's weird.
AI models are at one time getting bigger,
but also some of the smaller models are getting smarter and better.
So that's also in our advantage, meaning we can take bigger reasoning models
that traditionally have been maybe
a trillion parameters or whatever, billions of parameters.
These can be quantised now into some smaller models
that we can run onboard that are also smarter
than they were previously, a year or two ago.
So we have models getting smaller and smarter,
and we have compute getting better.
So those trends in general are in our favour,
and today we're running robots fully onboard,
so we don't have to wait for like...
"Let's hope compute catches up or models get some point in space."
We can do it all today.
So we really, today, have a path where, fully onboard,
we can run robots daily now in commercial operations.
The big bottleneck for us is we're--
We're trying to solve general robotics here at Figure.
And that for us means putting a robot into a home it's never been in before,
and just do a speech prompt, a voice prompt, ask it to do something.
This could be like, "I'm gonna be gone all day,
make sure my laundry's done before I get home."
And you want that task done and done correctly
over like a very long time horizon.
If I were to think every task, the ingredient or the input is energy.
Like, me, as a human, I can eat a sandwich
and I can do X amount because I can convert that into...
I can convert the energy of that sandwich into something.
Do you think the robot or the humanoid
ever gets to that point where it becomes more efficient
than a human in utilising energy or creating energy?
Today, we're far less efficient than a human.
Humans are-- we are extremely efficient.
Our biological neural nets are really efficient.
The way-- how much energy or how much power we use
to exert, like walking and movements and things, or--
and even just sitting here idle
is much better than we get on the robot today.
I see no reason, over time...
We're seeing power drop now across our robot systems.
There's a bunch of work that we're doing now.
There's the computational work to get more efficient.
And then there's the mechanical work
to get more efficient overall that we're looking at.
Both of those two are--
We're getting improvements on year to year, robot to robot.
I think we will approach...
We see a path approaching human-like energy consumption.
It's gonna be really hard.
You're talking to us now in the early days of this.
It's way more, like, 16 years ago.
It's early, it's just now happening.
We... There's a lot of stuff on our roadmap
that we're like, "Oh, man, we gotta go solve,"
manufacturing at scale, all these different things.
And reducing power footprint is certainly one of them.
I wouldn't say it's a blocker today.
We can do work in a lot of cases,
almost the whole day, depending on what the task looks like.
And if it's not that,
if it's a task that has a lot of locomotion and movements,
we can do human shifts in terms of time span.
Is that the ultimate test?
If a robot were to have general intelligence
with the same amount of energy,
it should be able to do not more of one task,
but more of any task a human can do?
Yeah, I think ultimately, yeah.
We would maybe judge this on
how much Watts it really took to do these longer horizon tasks.
I think the near-term thing is,
if you've got a robot in your home and you speak and you voice prompt it,
what percentage of those things can they do fully and like a human?
And learn from those mistakes.
We make a lot of-- We do a lot of planning.
Like doing laundry, it's like, "Okay, well, where're the laundry baskets?"
Or, "What is the laundry?"
"Oh, they're in the rooms." "Which rooms?"
"Okay, this room upstairs or downstairs."
And you have to do a bunch of really quick,
probably subconscious planning that's happening.
And then we gotta go out from actually,
almost serially complete those tasks.
There's a lot of local failures that are happening.
You go to a room and there's a laundry basket there.
What do you do?
A robot's gotta be able to reason through that whole thing from A to Z.
And then in some cases, 'cause for laundry, it could take hours.
The ultimate test right now is,
can we get a humanoid robot to do those type of things?
And we've seen that in LLMs.
We've seen a robustness
to almost human-like intelligence that we're tapping into.
And we're trying to do that now in robotics.
How do we get this machine to reason about the world?
Have human common sense.
And I think that would just be like-- That'll be incredible.
When we hit that, you're gonna have a robot in your home
and you're gonna be doing this and it's just gonna be incredible.
And then from there, yeah, getting to more efficient systems, getting the cost down.
I think you're gonna spend a lot of time with the robots.
As almost like a companion.
They're gonna be in your home. They're gonna be with your kids.
They're gonna be-- they're gonna know everything.
They're gonna have almost this infinite context window for memory.
I think you're gonna talk to it and it's gonna know if you...
Emotionally, from an EQ perspective, it's gonna know
if you've had a tough day from just the sound of your voice
when you come home.
And I think there's a really important like--
Do you program? Do you program EQ through...
How do you teach a robot to have EQ?
Does it measure your voice every day and you have to give it data
as to, "This day I was feeling a certain way." It learns like that?
The best AI,
calling native audio voice reasoning models today
can do a decent job at really understanding EQ well.
I would say it hasn't done as well with...
I think IQ has been a big focus for the whole space now
of information retrieval for voice models and things.
But there's a real path to getting voice models to really high IQ.
And that can be understanding your tone,
and your emotional state when talking.
These are fully solvable things.
It's in the world model, it's in the data sets.
And it's a matter of accurately training
new AI, voice and reasoning models,
to be able to understand this well and do well at this.
But this is a solvable thing.
We were spending time on it here, internally.
I would say the next year or two, you will have voice...
AI models that you're talking to, whether it's on the phone
or it could be through a robot or whatever,
that you really won't be able to tell if it's a human or machine anymore.
And going back to the robot,
you said there are 40 joints, 40 motors, eight cameras.
The torso has the GPU and the battery.
What else is in it?
We have an order of a thousand parts.
So it's, it's not just... I...
As impetuous as, say, here are the things--
But the big stuff, what's in the head?
The head has cameras.
We have the normal network connections
you would want, like 5G, Wi-Fi, Bluetooth.
We have in Figure 03, we have three screens.
We have a front screen, like an OLED.
We have two side screens.
We can-- I'll talk about that.
We have a speaker and mic, a new speaker and mic system.
That's really, actually really good for Figure 03.
So we're spending a lot more time speaking to the robots.
I think voice is gonna be the natural UI into the robot.
You're not--
Maybe you have an app on your phone or whatever,
but voice is how you're gonna communicate most of the time through the robot.
And if you're not with it, you'll text it.
That is gonna be such an important part of the ecosystem here for--
Is that the new form factor for AI overall? To communicate with AI?
'Cause a lot of people have been extrapolating
what could be the next form factor after the cell phone.
Do you think it would be something voice related?
How does a man communicate with a computer tomorrow?
Yeah, my view is that voice is the natural UI
into artificial super intelligence here, in general,
whether it's like a digital systems or physical systems.
And voice is gonna play an extremely important role in all of this.
I think you will have both language devices,
almost next generation, kind of iPhone level products.
And I think you will have humanoids.
And I think they will be networked really well together.
Whenever I say this to my friends,
they ask me, "If you're sitting in a meeting, you can't speak...
to interact with your device, but you can pull up a phone."
Yeah, the reality is the...
There's a couple of things, as we think about the next frontier of devices:
The first is speech is just not good enough today.
Our voice models are not good enough today
for us to really understand how
this could be ubiquitous and it's the main UI.
They're just-- they're B, they need to be A-plus.
They're not good enough in a lot of areas.
They talk over you, you can't interject.
Sometimes the IQ gets collapsed in training,
and it's just not very intelligent.
And when tool calling, there's a bunch of random issues,
but that is gonna get really good over the next 12 to 18 months.
What we will see in 12 to 18 months,
voice models approaching human Turing tests.
And that's not just from an IQ perspective and information retrieval,
it's EQ as well, as we've just talked about naturalness.
So I think-- That's one.
Two is the models today just don't have enough context.
We're missing the... When we're going out in the world
and we're talking today, all this conduct,
it's being missed, the models don't understand this.
What it's forcing us to do is it's making us go
to a computer and type in prompts and ask it things
and tell it, "Hey, I have this thing and please help me here."
But if you had all this information, you had a limited memory
and retrieval of all this information we're having
all throughout the day, every day,
these AI systems would need very little
understanding of what is currently in context
or what you need to do right now to help you.
My view is the next generation of stuff
is basically language devices and humanoid robots.
And that is-- that'll go extremely far, in my mind.
Will they replace the traditional phone or computer?
Yes.
Every phone and computer was built pre-AI.
All the hardware we have today in the world
needs to be completely rebuilt from scratch.
Think about it like this.
We have human-like intelligence in the digital format now.
And we are communicating with this through really old pre-AI devices.
There's zero chance or very low chance
that they were somehow built specifically well enough for this new technology.
In my mind, they're not optimised well enough for this.
So you need a whole new class of devices.
And robotics is a part of this story
to basically be able to take this...
What's happening right now is we have this massive AI overhang
where AI models are able to do incredible things
in, I would call it, the tech community.
We have humanoid robots, and incredible things with AI.
I can ask computer-using agents to do crazy things now
on computers without any human involvement.
And then most of the world is using AI systems
for information retrieval, like a Google search.
And it's mostly a constraint on hardware at this point.
So, this is a digression, Brett, but I was investing in
a hardware company recently called Nothing, the phone company.
And give me some advice.
I was playing off this thesis that a lot of the large language models
are getting very democratised, and the world will end up at a point
where they're all a commodity and they're very similar to each other
